what:
A MySQL Language Pack for OpenFn. The adaptor lets OpenFn workflows read from and write to MySQL.

It exposes 4 operations that you can call from a workflow step, listed in the Operations tab. 4 ready-to-paste snippets are grouped as create (2) and search (2). Vendor homepage: https://www.mysql.com.
connect:
Oracle supports HTTP Basic auth for API access.

The credential schema asks for host (required), port, database (required), user (required), and password (required). Auth reference: https://dev.mysql.com/doc/refman/8.0/en/connecting.html.
when:
Reach for this adaptor when your workflow needs to read or write data in MySQL.

5 triggers are catalogued (0 webhook, 5 polling), including New Column, New or Updated Row, New Row, New Row (Custom Query), and more. See the Triggers tab for the full list. Oracle doesn't appear to expose webhooks, so plan on polling at a sensible interval.
wire:
A typical wiring pattern is trigger → fetch → transform → write.

Kick the workflow off from a trigger on the Triggers tab (polled on a cron). Then chain operations from this adaptor's Operations tab to fetch or write data in Oracle. Pass values between steps through state.data — every operation reads from and writes to job state.

To move data between systems, follow this step with another adaptor (e.g. write the fetched records into a downstream FHIR, DHIS2, or database adaptor).
scale:
When running Oracle jobs at volume:

- Oracle doesn't surface webhooks, so poll on the longest interval your SLA allows and use a since-cursor to avoid re-reading everything.
- Batch requests where the API supports it and avoid fan-out loops over large collections.
- Make jobs idempotent by keying on stable external IDs so a replay doesn't create duplicates.
- Surface 4xx/5xx responses with the vendor request ID in your logs, and treat 429s as retries with backoff.

Adapter note: Generic database driver adapter — no vendor REST API. Surface is SQL via the mysqljs/mysql driver over the MySQL wire protocol.
best:
- Store Oracle credentials in OpenFn's credential store; never inline keys or tokens in job code.
- Pin @openfn/language-mysql to v4.0.5 in package.json so an upstream change doesn't break a production workflow silently.
- Keep each step small — one logical API call per operation makes failures easier to diagnose and retry.
- Add defensive checks before transforming inbound payloads — vendor responses change between versions.
- Log enough context (resource id, run id, vendor request id) so an incident can be traced end-to-end.
